stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{3} + ([[a]])x + ([[b]]) \), a continuous function. By the Intermediate Value Theorem, \( f \) is guaranteed to have a root between \( x = [[p]] \) and \( x = [[q]] \) exactly when \( f([[p]])\cdot f([[q]]) < 0 \). Compute \( f([[p]])\cdot f([[q]]) \).
solution: Evaluate the endpoints: \( f([[p]]) = [[p]]^{3} + ([[a]])([[p]]) + ([[b]]) \) and \( f([[q]]) = [[q]]^{3} + ([[a]])([[q]]) + ([[b]]) \). Their product is \( [[answer]] \). Since it is negative, \( f \) changes sign on \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \), so the Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees a root there.
distractor_rationales: This adds the endpoint values instead of multiplying them.
This is the absolute value (sign dropped); the sign is exactly what signals a root.
This is only \( f([[p]]) \), a single endpoint value.
This is only \( f([[q]]) \), a single endpoint value.
This is off by one from the correct product.
This is off by one from the correct product.

stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{3} + ([[a]])x + ([[b]]) \), continuous on \( [[[p]], [[q]]] \). By the Intermediate Value Theorem, \( f \) must attain the value \( M = \dfrac{f([[p]]) + f([[q]])}{2} \), the average of its endpoint values, at some point in \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \) (since \( M \) lies between \( f([[p]]) \) and \( f([[q]]) \)). Compute \( M \).
solution: Evaluate the endpoints: \( f([[p]]) = [[p]]^{3} + ([[a]])([[p]]) + ([[b]]) \) and \( f([[q]]) = [[q]]^{3} + ([[a]])([[q]]) + ([[b]]) \). Their average is \( M = [[answer]] \); since \( M \) lies between the two endpoint values, the Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees some \( c \in ([[p]], [[q]]) \) with \( f(c) = M \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the endpoint values instead of averaging them.
This is only \( f([[p]]) \), a single endpoint value.
This is only \( f([[q]]) \), a single endpoint value.
This forgets to divide the sum by 2.
This is off by one from the correct average.
This is off by one from the correct average.

stem: Let \( f(x) = x^{3} + ([[a]])x \) and \( g(x) = ([[c]])x + ([[d]]) \), both continuous. Define \( h(x) = f(x) - g(x) \). By the Intermediate Value Theorem, the graphs of \( f \) and \( g \) are guaranteed to cross somewhere in \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \) exactly when \( h([[p]])\cdot h([[q]]) < 0 \). Compute \( h([[p]])\cdot h([[q]]) \).
solution: Let \( h(x) = f(x) - g(x) = x^{3} + ([[a]] - [[c]])x - ([[d]]) \). Evaluating, \( h([[p]]) = [[p]]^{3} + ([[a]])([[p]]) - ([[c]])([[p]]) - ([[d]]) \) and \( h([[q]]) = [[q]]^{3} + ([[a]])([[q]]) - ([[c]])([[q]]) - ([[d]]) \); their product is \( [[answer]] \). Since it is negative, \( h \) changes sign, so by IVT \( h \) has a root in \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \) — the point where \( f \) and \( g \) intersect.
distractor_rationales: This adds the endpoint values of \( h \) instead of multiplying them.
This is the absolute value (sign dropped); the sign is exactly what signals an intersection.
This is only \( h([[p]]) \), a single endpoint value.
This is only \( h([[q]]) \), a single endpoint value.
This is off by one from the correct product.
This is off by one from the correct product.

stem: Let \( f(x) = e^{x} - ([[b]])x - ([[c]]) \), a continuous function. By the Intermediate Value Theorem, \( f \) is guaranteed to have a root between \( x = [[p]] \) and \( x = [[q]] \) exactly when \( f([[p]])\cdot f([[q]]) < 0 \). Compute \( f([[p]])\cdot f([[q]]) \).
solution: Evaluate the endpoints: \( f([[p]]) = e^{[[p]]} - ([[b]])([[p]]) - ([[c]]) \) and \( f([[q]]) = e^{[[q]]} - ([[b]])([[q]]) - ([[c]]) \). Their product is \( [[answer]] \). Since it is negative, \( f \) changes sign on \( ([[p]], [[q]]) \), so the Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees a root there.
distractor_rationales: This adds the endpoint values instead of multiplying them.
This is the absolute value (sign dropped); the sign is exactly what signals a root.
This is only \( f([[p]]) \), a single endpoint value.
This is only \( f([[q]]) \), a single endpoint value.
